MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Nov-14-2019 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
James C. Parker of HUGO & PARKER (415-808-0300), appearing for defendant and moving party Merlex Stucco, Inc.
Douglas V. Dukelow of KELLER, FISHBACK & JACKSON LLP (818-342-7442), appearing for plaintiff and opposing party Gordon Andersen.

RULING: DEFENDANT MERLEX STUCCO, INC.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, OR IN THE ALTERNATIVE, SUMMARY ADJUDICATION

Hearing held, not argued and tentative ruling adopted: Defendant Merlex Stucco, Inc.'s Motion for Summary Judgment, or in the Alternative, Summary Adjudication is DENIED. Defendant failed to meet its initial burden of demonstrating that Plaintiff does not possess and cannot reasonably obtain evidence that he was exposed to asbestos-containing products or materials attributable to Defendant. (Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826.) Defendant failed to show that it conducted comprehensive written discovery designed to elicit all the evidence Plaintiff has to support his claim of exposure. (Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2006) 143 Cal. App. 4th 1433, 1442.) Plaintiff's objection to Exhibits B and C of the amended declaration of Daniel Bales is sustained. Defendant's motion for summary adjudication as to negligence, strict liability, false representation, intentional tort, and punitive damages is denied on the same grounds as Defendant relies on the same undisputed material facts and evidence used in support of its motion for summary judgment. Order signed in open court. Judge: Cynthia Ming-mei Lee, Clerk: A. Mok. Not reported. (503/CML)

Off Calendar Nov-08-2019 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
Stephen M. Fishback of KELLER, FISHBACK & JACKSON LLP (818-342-7442), appearing for plaintiff and moving party Gordon Andersen.
Duncan S. Lemmon and Shayan Heidarzadeh of FOLEY & MANSFIELD (510-590-9500), appearing for defendant and opposing party Ameron International Corporation.

RULING: PLAINTIFF'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION OF DEFENDANT AMERON INTERNATIONAL CORPORATION'S AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSES

Hearing held, argued and tentative ruling adopted: Plaintiff's Motion for Summary Adjudication of Defendant Ameron International Corporation's Affirmative Defenses is DENIED. Court did not consider plaintiff and defendant's late supplemental declarations. Defendant has withdrawn defenses nos. 2, 3, 4, 5, 8, 9, 12, 18, 22, 23, 28, 29, 30, 33, 34, 36, 43, 47, 48, 49, and 50. As to all remaining defenses, Plaintiff failed to meet its burden of demonstrating that Defendant does not possess and cannot reasonably obtain evidence regarding its affirmative defenses. (Defendant's Ex. O [Defendant's third supplemental responses to Plaintiff's special interrogatories nos. 30-32]; Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 843, 850-51.) The motion is denied as to all defenses on the same grounds as Plaintiff relies on undisputed material facts 1-10 and corresponding evidence for each defense. Prevailing party is to submit a proposed order in compliance with C.R.C. 3.1312. Judge: Cynthia Ming-mei Lee, Clerk: A. Mok. Reporter: Kelly Roemer of AIKEN WELCH, CSR # 6435, kroemer@aikenwelch.com (Tel: 877-451-1580) (503/CML)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION And Notice Of Motion
Off Calendar Nov-06-2019 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
Gardiner McKleroy of WILSON ELSER MOSKOWITZ EDELMAN & DICKER LLP (415-433-0990), appearing for defendant and moving party Defendant Golden Gate Drywall.
Stephen M. Fishback of KELLER, FISHBACK & JACKSON LLP (818-342-7442), appearing for plaintiff and opposing party Gordon Andersen.

RULING: DEFENDANT GOLDEN GATE DRYWALL'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION AS TO PLAINTIFF GORDON ANDERSEN'S CLAIM FOR PUNITIVE DAMAGES
Hearing held, argued and tentative ruling adopted: Defendant Golden Gate Drywall's Motion for Summary Adjudication as to Plaintiff Gordon Andersen's Claim for Punitive Damages is GRANTED. Defendant sustained its burden of showing that Plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain needed evidence that Defendant acted with intent, malice, or oppression. (Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 855). Plaintiff's responses to Defendant's special interrogatories are factually devoid as it contains boilerplate answers that restate Plaintiff's allegations and laundry lists of people and/or documents. (Defendant's Ex. E [Plaintiff's responses to special interrogatory nos. 22-24].)

Plaintiff failed to submit sufficient evidence to create a triable issue whether Defendant acted with the requisite malice, fraud or oppression to warrant the imposition of punitive damages. The depositions of Stephen Eckstrom and Matthew Thompson, and declaration of Richard Cohen, M.D., M.P.H. are not sufficient to create a triable issue as they do not demonstrate a willful and conscious disregard of the rights or safety of others. (Plaintiff's Exs. B and C)

While Plaintiff does not need to prove his case for punitive damages at the summary adjudication stage, the Court takes the higher proof standard into consideration and concludes that no reasonable jury could find Plaintiff's evidence to be clear and convincing proof of malice, fraud or oppression. (See American Airlines, Inc. v. Sheppard, Mullin, Richter & Hampton (2002) 96 Cal.App.4th 1017, 1049; Hoch v. Allied-Signal, Inc. (1994) 24 Cal.App.4th 48, 60-61.) Order signed in open court. Judge: Cynthia Ming-mei Lee, Clerk: A. Mok. Reporter: Kimberly L. Avery of AIKEN WELCH, CSR #5074 (Tel: 877-451-1580) (503/CML)

Off Calendar Nov-06-2019 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
Robert G. Engel of WILSON ELSER MOSKOWITZ EDELMAN & DICKER LLP (415-433-0990), appearing for defendant and moving party Defendant A.H. Voss Company.
Diran Tashjian of KELLER, FISHBACK & JACKSON LLP (818-342-7442), appearing for plaintiff and opposing party Gordon Andersen.

RULING: DEFENDANT A.H. VOSS COMPANY'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION AS TO PLAINTIFF GORDON ANDERSEN'S CLAIM FOR PUNITIVE DAMAGES
Hearing held, argued and tentative ruling adopted: Defendant A.H. Voss Company's Motion for Summary Adjudication as to Plaintiff Gordon Andersen's Claim for Punitive Damages is GRANTED. Defendant sustained its burden of showing that Plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain needed evidence that Defendant acted with intent, malice, or oppression. (Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 855). Plaintiff's responses to Defendant's special interrogatories are factually devoid as it contains boilerplate answers that restate Plaintiff's allegations and laundry lists of people and/or documents. (Defendant's Ex. E [Plaintiff's response to special interrogatory no. 23].) Plaintiff's objection to Plaintiff's Responses to A.H. Voss Company's Special Interrogatories is overruled. The Court exercises its discretion to consider the verifications to Plaintiff's interrogatory responses, which were executed by Plaintiff on July 12, 2018. (Passalacqua Supplemental Declaration at Ex. A; Jacobs v. Coldwell Banker Residential Brokerage Co. (2017) 14 Cal. App. 5th 438, 449.)

Plaintiff failed to submit sufficient evidence to create a triable issue whether Defendant acted with the requisite malice, fraud or oppression to warrant the imposition of punitive damages. The depositions of Stephen Eckstrom and declaration of Richard Cohen, M.D., M.P.H. are not sufficient to create a triable issue as they do not demonstrate a willful and conscious disregard of the rights or safety of others. (Plaintiff's Ex. C)

While Plaintiff does not need to prove his case for punitive damages at the summary adjudication stage, the Court takes the higher proof standard into consideration and concludes that no reasonable jury could find Plaintiff's evidence to be clear and convincing proof of malice, fraud or oppression. (See American Airlines, Inc. v. Sheppard, Mullin, Richter & Hampton (2002) 96 Cal.App.4th 1017, 1049; Hoch v. Allied-Signal, Inc. (1994) 24 Cal.App.4th 48, 60-61.) Order signed in open court. Judge: Cynthia Ming-mei Lee, Clerk: A. Mok. Reporter: Kimberly L. Avery of AIKEN WELCH, CSR #5074 (Tel: 877-451-1580) (503/CML)

Diran Tashjian of KELLER, FISHBACK & JACKSON LLP (818-564-5135), appearing for plaintiff and opposing party Gordon Andersen.

RULING: DEFENDANT VWR INTERNATIONAL LLC'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, OR IN THE ALTERNATIVE, SUMMARY ADJUDICATION
Hearing held, argued and tentative ruling adopted: Defendant VWR International LLC's Motion for Summary Judgment, or in the Alternative, Summary Adjudication is DENIED. Defendant sustained its initial burden of demonstrating that Plaintiff does not possess and cannot reasonably obtain evidence that he was exposed to asbestos-containing products or materials attributable to Defendant. (Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826.) The declarations of Gordon Andersen and Charles Ay create a triable issue of fact as to whether Plaintiff was exposed to asbestos as a result of his use of protective clothing including the gloves, mittens, arm coverings, aprons, and suits supplied by Defendant. Defendant's objections to the declarations of Gordon Andersen and Charles Ay are overruled. As to Defendant's alternate motion for summary adjudication, it is denied in its entirety for the same reasons stated above. For each summary adjudication issue, Defendant relies on the same set of undisputed material facts used in support of its motion for summary judgment. Order signed in chambers. MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION As To Plaintiff Gordon Andersen S Claim For Punitive Damages
Off Calendar Oct-17-2019 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
Amy M. Kott of SINUNU BRUNI, LLP (415-362-9700), appearing for defendant and moving party California Drywall Company.
Bruce Jackson of KELLER, FISHBACK & JACKSON LLP (818-342-7442), appearing for plaintiff and opposing party Gordon Andersen.

RULING: DEFENDANT CALIFORNIA DRYWALL COMPANY'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION AS TO PLAINTIFF GORDON ANDERSEN'S CLAIM FOR PUNITIVE DAMAGES

Hearing held, argued and tentative ruling adopted: Defendant California Drywall Company's Motion for Summary Adjudication as to Plaintiff Gordon Andersen's Claim for Punitive Damages is GRANTED. The Court did not consider Defendant's reply papers for failure to lodge courtesy copies with the Court. (Local Rule 2.7(B).) Defendant sustained its initial burden via the factually devoid prong of Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826. Plaintiff's special interrogatory responses demonstrate that Plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, evidence that Defendant acted with the requisite malice, fraud or oppression to warrant the imposition of punitive damages. (Exs. D, F to Kott Declaration [Plaintiff's responses to Special Interrogatory Nos. 24-27 including Exhibit A to response No. 25].) Plaintiff's objection to paragraph 3 of the Kott Declaration regarding Plaintiff's response in Exhibit A is overruled. Plaintiff failed to submit sufficient evidence to create a triable issue whether Defendant acted with the requisite malice, fraud or oppression to warrant the imposition of punitive damages. The deposition of Stephen Eckstrom and declaration of Richard Cohen, M.D., M.P.H. are not sufficient to create a triable issue as they do not demonstrate a willful and conscious disregard of the rights or safety of others. (Exs. B and D to Tashjian Declaration.) While Plaintiff does not need to prove his case for punitive damages at the summary adjudication stage, the Court takes the higher proof standard into consideration and concludes that no reasonable jury could find Plaintiff's evidence to be clear and convincing proof of malice, fraud or oppression. (See American Airlines, Inc. v. Sheppard, Mullin, Richter & Hampton (2002) 96 Cal.App.4th 1017, 1049; Hoch v. Allied-Signal, Inc. (1994) 24 Cal.App.4th 48, 60-61.) Order signed in open court. RULING: DEFENDANT AMERON INTERNATIONAL CORPORATION'S MOTION TO COMPEL INSPECTION OF LAND OR PREMISES AND REQUEST FOR MONETARY AND ISSUE SANCTIONS AGAINST PLAINTIFFS AND THEIR ATTORNEYS
Hearing held, argued and the Court ruled as follows: Defendant Ameron International Corporation's motion to compel inspection of land or premises and request for monetary and issue sanctions against plaintiffs and their attorneys is granted in part. Defendant's request to inspect interior of plaintiff's residence in Texas is granted, excluding the bedroom(s) of non-plaintiff residents. The Court declined to rule on the issues of sanctions as it was premature. Compliance for inspection of home to be completed no later than April 16, 2019. Defendant to give notice for site inspection. Prevailing party is to submit a proposed order in compliance with C.R.C. 3.1312. Judge: Cynthia Ming-mei Lee, Clerk: A. Mok. Not reported. (503/CML)

RULING: PLAINTIFF'S MOTION FOR TRIAL PREFERENCE
Hearing held, argued and tentative ruling adopted: Plaintiff's motion for trial preference is denied without prejudice. Plaintiff fails to demonstrate that Mr. Andersen's health is such that preference is necessary to prevent prejudicing his interest in the litigation, as required by C.C.P. section 36(a). Plaintiff also fails to provide the court with clear and convincing medical documentation demonstrating substantial medical doubt that Mr. Andersen will survival beyond six months, as required by C.C.P section 36(d). Plaintiff also fails to demonstrate that the interests of justice will be served by granting preference, as required by C.C.P. section 36(e). Order signed in open court. Judge: Garrett L. Wong, Clerk: A. Mok. Reporter: Angela Sinclair of AIKEN WELCH, CSR # 13902, asinclair@aikenwelch.com (Tel: 510-451-1580). (503/GLW)

Off Calendar May-24-2018 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
Douglas V. Dukelow of KELLER, FISHBACK & JACKSON LLP (818-342-7442), appearing for plaintiff and moving party Gordon Andersen.
Emily Weissenberger of SHOOK, HARDY & BACON LLP (415-544-1900), appearing for defendants and opposing parties HP, Inc. and Hewlett Packard Enterprise Company.

RULING: NOTICE OF MOTION AND MOTION TO COMPEL HP, INC. AND HEWLETT-PACKARD ENTERPRISE COMPANY TO COMPLY WITH DEPOSITION SUBPOENA FOR PRODUCTION OF BUSINESS RECORDS

Hearing held, argued and tentative ruling amended as follows: Plaintiffs' motion to compel HP, Inc. and Hewlett-Packard Enterprise Company to comply with Deposition Subpoena for Production of Business Records is granted. HP, Inc. and Hewlett-Packard Enterprise Company must produce all documents responsive to Plaintiff's subpoena, subject to the limitations specified by Plaintiff in his reply papers, at least five days before Plaintiff's deposition and subject to the further limitations agreed to in plaintiff's May 2, 2018 letter attached here described in categories 1 and 2. The requested documents are not protected material, as Plaintiff states that he seeks information regarding the support systems delivering water, steam, and chemicals to different parts of the HP facilities. (Reply at 4.) Further, the categories of documents are relevant to Plaintiff's claims. Order signed in open court. Judge: Lynn O'Malley Taylor, Reporter: Katy Leonard, CSR #11599. (503/LOT)
